event_blocks.summary: The IAAF World Championships London 2017 Men's Long Jump Final commenced with a scoreboard display, followed by commentators setting the scene for the field event, emphasizing Luvo Manyonga's season-leading 8.65m jump and the challenges of qualification. A ceremonial fanfare was performed by uniformed musicians in red and black attire with gold helmets, adding pageantry before the athletes were introduced. The packed stadium, adorned with IAAF banners and '#BE THE NEXT,' buzzed with crowd noise and applause. Athletes were then presented: Fabrice Lapierre (Australia, PB 8.40m, SB 8.03m), Luvo Manyonga (South Africa, PB/SB 8.65m), Maykel Massó (Cuba, PB/SB 8.33m), Jarrion Lawson (USA, PB 8.58m, SB 8.33m), Ruswahl Samaai (South Africa, PB/SB 8.49m), Radek Juška (Czech Republic, PB/SB 8.29m), and Jianan Wang (China, PB/SB 8.29m). Each athlete, in their respective national colors, raised an arm with a distinct expression, under the bright evening stadium lights, amidst continuous crowd noise and commentary, with various sponsors' banners visible.

Ceremonial Musicians.description: Uniformed performers in red and black ceremonial attire with ornate gold helmets, white gloves, and brass instruments, playing a distinct, building fanfare on the track during the event's opening ceremony, which commentators describe as 'pageantry' before the final.

Fabrice Lapierre.description: Australian long jumper Fabrice Lapierre, a former world indoor champion now 33 years of age, is seen with a focused expression in his teal and yellow singlet with the Australian flag and 'AUSTRALIA' printed on it, as commentary highlights his career and his personal best (PB) of 8.40m and season best (SB) of 8.03m displayed on screen.

Scoreboard.description: A digital display showing the final standings of the Long Jump Men's event, listing athletes by rank, name, nationality, and country code, with positions 1–9 initially shown, then 10–12, while commentators discuss the 'field event final,' highlight 'South Africans' and 'Manyonga' with his 'massive 8.65' jump, and mention '12 athletes in the final.'

Stadium Crowd.description: A massive, densely packed audience in the stands, visible during wide shots, creating a vibrant and energetic atmosphere with advertising banners for IAAF.org, TDK, Toyota, and Hydrogen Fuel Cell, while contributing to the overall stadium buzz with their presence and sounds.

Maykel Massó.description: Cuban long jumper Maykel Massó, who is still a junior at only 18 years of age and was fourth in the world and Youth Junior Championships, raises his arm with a determined look in his red singlet with 'CUBA' and the Cuban flag, his PB and SB at 8.33m.

Jianan Wang.description: Chinese long jumper Jianan Wang, noted by commentary as one of the good Chinese athletes, raises his arm with a calm demeanor in his red singlet with 'CHINA' and the Chinese flag, his PB and SB both at 8.29m.

Luvo Manyonga.description: South African long jumper Luvo Manyonga, described by commentary as 'the big man himself' and the Commonwealth record holder with a PB and SB of 8.65m, raises his arms in celebration while wearing his green and yellow singlet with 'SOUTH AFRICA' and the national emblem.

Jarrion Lawson.description: American long jumper Jarrion Lawson, a former sprinter who won the 100m, 200m, long jump, and triple jump in collegiate championships, raises his arm confidently in his blue Nike singlet with 'USA' and the American flag, his PB at 8.58m and SB at 8.33m.

Ruswahl Samaai.description: South African long jumper Ruswahl Samaai, identified by commentary as another strong contender who could produce an upset, smiles broadly and raises his arms in his green and yellow singlet with 'SOUTH AFRICA' and the national emblem, his PB and SB both at 8.49m.

Radek Juška.description: Czech long jumper Radek Juška (mispronounced as Jusek by the commentator), who produced the biggest jump in qualifying at 8.24m and was a world bronze medalist two years ago, raises his arm with a composed expression in his red singlet with 'CZECH REPUBLIC' and the Czech flag, his PB and SB both at 8.29m.

event_blocks.summary: The IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017 long jump competition commenced with athletes like Damar Forbes of Jamaica, Aleksandr Menkov of the ANA, Michel Tornéus of Sweden, Emiliano Lasa of Uruguay, and Yuhao Shi of China being introduced, with commentators noting their personal bests and season bests while expressing uncertainty about their current form. Fabrice Lapierre of Australia, a former World Indoor Champion with an impressive 8.65m jump this year, was also highlighted, alongside potential contender Steve Man Yonga. The stadium buzzed with anticipation, visible through the packed stands, 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banners, and the cheers of the crowd. Lapierre, after adjusting his attire and with his PB of 8.40m and SB of 8.03m displayed, executed his first jump. Despite a powerful sprint and a good takeoff, as indicated by a low-angle shot and a female official waving a white flag, the jump was measured at 7.89 meters with a +0.7 m/s wind reading, placing him first but falling short of expectations. Commentators observed a lack of speed in his approach, despite good height, and emphasized the importance of a strong early jump for advancement. Ceremonial musicians in red and black uniforms with gold helmets played bugles, adding to the grand ambiance as the competition began.

event_blocks.summary: Aleksandr Menkov, wearing a blue and pink Nike singlet, held second place with a jump of 8.27m, surpassing Samai and trailing Lawson, as confirmed by commentators and the scoreboard. Later, Manyonga, in his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, was confirmed to have sailed into first place with a "massive 8.48" jump. American long jumper Jarrion Lawson, in a blue Nike 'USA' singlet, prepared intensely for his second-round jump, his "tension building up, spring is being coiled." Lawson's powerful run and leap into the sandpit, marked by a splash, were met with palpable crowd anticipation. An official raised a white flag, signaling a valid jump, confirmed by commentators as "goes up again!" The scoreboard showed Lawson's result as 8.37m, a Season Best, ranking him second, prompting questions if it would surpass Manyonga's 8.48m. The stadium, adorned with banners like 'WELCOME TO LONDON', 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017', featured close-ups of Lawson's focus and celebratory gestures, alongside crowd reactions, including a Spectator in Teal 'AUSTRALIA' Jacket and a Man in Blue Hoodie with 'AMERICAN FREEDOM'. He performs a strong jump, landing with a splash, and the scoreboard displays his 8.43m (SB) jump, securing his second-place position, which the commentator confirms, highlighting the 'preciously close' competition with 'centimeters separating everybody.' The stadium crowd is seen applauding and cheering throughout, adding to the high-stakes atmosphere, with prominent banners for 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and sponsors like 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'TDK' visible.

The segment opens with Jarrion Lawson, the American long jumper in his blue Nike singlet, intensely preparing for his jump, adjusting his hair with a characteristic "shaking leg" and leaning back, as noted by the commentators. He then executes a powerful run-up along the red track, described as "fast into the board," launching himself with a strong leap into the sandpit, creating a visible splash of sand. The commentators initially assess it as "a big jump," but express doubt it will "threaten Manyonga." A female official at the white and blue takeoff board promptly raises a white flag, confirming a valid jump, a moment highlighted by the commentator stating, "The white flag goes up." As Lawson walks away, the scoreboard updates, initially showing an incorrect reading before settling on his season best of 8.43m, placing him in second position, "just behind Manyonga" who leads with 8.48m. Commentators analyze his technique, noting he was "a long way back from the board," potentially losing "precious distance." The stadium is brightly lit, with banners like 'Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park' visible, and the massive crowd, whose cheers and clapping are clearly audible, remains engaged, with spectators like a man in a red 'ARKANSAS' hoodie watching intently. The scoreboard further details Lawson's series of jumps and a wind reading of +0.5, as the commentators refer to him as "the big man" in this electric atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: The commentators anticipated a massive jump from Luvo Manyonga, who executed a powerful run-up and leap, initially deemed disappointing, but a female official raised a white flag, confirming a valid attempt of 8.48 meters, securing his first-place position as the crowd cheered, including a man in a black polo shirt with a lanyard and another in a red 'ARKANSAS' hoodie. The commentator highlighted the historical significance of a potential South African champion. A close-up showed Manyonga’s foot near the takeoff board with a +0.4 wind reading, his coach observing with anticipation. The illuminated Olympic Park displayed 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', 'TDK', and 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banners, with the scoreboard showing Manyonga, Lawson, and Samaai in the top three. Manyonga walked away focused, as the commentator emphasized his approach to the world title. Ruswahl Samaai, in his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, focused on his final attempt, his previous jumps displayed on the scoreboard, and after a powerful run-up and leap, he landed in the sandpit, prompting a white flag from an official, as the crowd cheered and recorded. Banners for 'SEIKO', 'TDK', 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017', and 'QUEEN ELIZABETH OLYMPIC PARK' were visible, with a replay showing his takeoff. Samaai then celebrated his 8.32 meter jump, securing third place behind Manyonga and Lawson, as Aleksandr Menkov, in a blue and pink Nike singlet, prepared, his inconsistent performance noted by the commentator. Menkov's jump resulted in a red flag, signaling a foul, leaving him frustrated as the scoreboard displayed the current standings. Lawson, in his blue Nike singlet, stood focused, with the commentator noting his potential for a bigger jump. Lawson performed his routine, energizing the crowd, and executed a powerful run-up and leap, resulting in a white flag and a jump of 8.44 meters, placing him in second. Manyonga knelt on the track. Samaai celebrated his bronze medal, while Lawson walked with the U.S. flag, and Manyonga prepared for his final jump, his anxiety noted by commentators. Manyonga executed a powerful run-up and leap, securing the gold medal with 8.48m, as the crowd roared and the scoreboard displayed the final standings. Manyonga celebrated his victory, joined by Samaai, embracing and waving the South African flag, surrounded by photographers, with trackside banners for 'TOYOTA' and 'TDK' visible, as the commentator reflected on Manyonga's inspiring journey. Manyonga, draped in the South African flag, high-fived Samaai, the on-screen graphic confirming his gold medal. The scoreboard displayed the final results, with the crowd cheering and recording, and banners for IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017, TOYOTA, and SEIKO visible.

raw_segments: The segment opens with commentators anticipating a "massive jump" from Luvo Manyonga, noting he's "been looking so, so good." Manyonga, in his green and yellow South Africa singlet, executes a powerful run-up and leap into the sandpit. Initially, a commentator expresses disappointment, saying, "Oh no, not as good as he thought he was going to do." However, a female official quickly raises a white flag, an action confirmed by the commentator, indicating a valid attempt. The scoreboard then displays his result of 8.48 meters, which the commentator confirms "won't improve on his 8.48 in the second round," but quickly adds, "Doesn't matter, still there in first place at the moment." The camera captures the excited, densely packed stadium crowd, audibly cheering, including close-ups of a man in a black polo shirt with a lanyard and another in a red 'ARKANSAS' hoodie, both visibly engaged. The commentator highlights the historical significance: "if he wins, he'll be the first South African champion in his event for Africa." A close-up of the takeoff board shows Manyonga’s foot just before takeoff, with a digital overlay indicating a +0.4 wind reading, while the commentator notes he was "a bit back from that plasticine." His coach is then shown, with the commentator speculating, "I think he knows there's a big jump there somewhere." The scene transitions to a wide aerial shot of the illuminated Olympic Park and the stadium at night, showcasing digital banners for 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'TDK', along with the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banner. The scoreboard updates to show Manyonga (8.48m), Lawson (8.43m), and Samaai (8.27m) in the top three. The segment concludes with Manyonga walking away, looking focused and composed, as the crowd continues to cheer, and the commentator emphasizes he's "closing in on the world title," reflecting on his "colorful" life story. The environment is electric with bright stadium lights and prominent 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banners.

The segment opens with a close-up of Ruswahl Samaai, the South African long jumper, wearing his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet with 'SAMAAL' and 'LONDON 2017' on his bib, his focused expression underscored by a commentator noting his "difficult routes" but that "he's bouncing back" to the "final four in the long jump," currently in "bronze medal position." The scoreboard displays his previous attempts: 8.25m, a foul (X), 8.15m, another foul (X), and his last valid jump of 8.27m, as the commentator highlights this is his "last attempt now." With intense concentration, Samaai begins his run-up, arms pumping as he accelerates down the red track. The camera follows his powerful stride towards the takeoff board, with the commentator anticipating the jump with an "Oh..." He leaps into the air with a strong, controlled jump, landing in the sandpit with a splash, as the commentator immediately declares, "It's a good jump, it really is." Officials in white shirts and black pants are visible near the pit, and one raises a white flag, which the commentator confirms by stating, "The white flag goes up." After landing, Samaai rises, claps his hands, and walks away with a composed demeanor, acknowledging the packed stadium crowd, who are clapping and cheering, some recording on phones, as the commentator notes, "He's thanking the crowd." Prominent banners like 'SEIKO', 'TDK', 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017', and 'QUEEN ELIZABETH OLYMPIC PARK' are visible. Wide shots emphasize the grand scale of the event and the illuminated roof. The segment concludes with a replay of Samaai’s jump from a low-angle perspective, highlighting his takeoff and flight over the sandpit, while the commentator provides further analysis, mentioning his previous 8.27m and the target of "8.43," adding that he was "3 centimeters behind the board" on this "good, good jump." The continuous background sound of the cheering crowd reinforces the vibrant atmosphere throughout.

The segment opens with Ruswahl Samaai, the South African long jumper in his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, celebrating triumphantly after a successful jump. A commentator confirms his "8:32" meter mark, exclaiming "Wow, marvelous, that's his best performance so far, best jump so far," which places him in "third place" and "the bronze medal at the moment" on the scoreboard, behind Luvo Manyonga (8.48m) and Jarrion Lawson (8.43m). The packed Stadium Crowd is illuminated under the night sky, with 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and 'TOYOTA' banners visible. The focus then shifts to Aleksandr Menkov, an athlete from ANA in a blue and pink Nike singlet, bright yellow and pink shoes, and a beaded necklace, walking with a focused expression. The commentator introduces him as "the only man who can deny him a place on the podium, Steve," but notes his "inconsistent card," detailing his "8:27 in his first around and then four no jumps." These "four no jumps have cost him dearly," the commentator explains, as he would have been in bronze medal position otherwise. Menkov prepares and executes a powerful run-up and leap into the sandpit. An official raises a red flag, signaling a foul jump, which the scoreboard immediately reflects as his fourth attempt. Menkov walks away with a neutral expression. The commentator emphasizes that "he's got to jump better than 8:32 to unsettle Samaai in that third place at the moment," before mentioning "Menkov" by name as he begins to describe the potential of the jump. 'SEIKO' and 'TDK' banners are visible along the trackside throughout the vibrant stadium atmosphere.

The segment opens with Aleksandr Menkov, in his blue and pink Nike singlet, bright yellow and pink shoes, and beaded necklace, landing in the sandpit after a long jump attempt. A commentator immediately notes, "Oh, it's a good jump but I don't think it's going to... oh, the red flag goes up," as the Red Flag Official raises a red flag, signaling a foul. Menkov walks away with a visibly disappointed expression, which the commentator attributes to his "fourth place for Menkov," calling it "the worst position... because you're so close to that podium." The camera follows his dejected demeanor along the track, showing the scoreboard displaying his results: 8.27m, followed by four fouls (X). He then sits on purple stadium seats, visibly frustrated, as the scoreboard updates with current standings: Luvo Manyonga in first with 8.48m, Jarrion Lawson in second with 8.43m, and Ruswahl Samaai in third with 8.32m. The commentator reads these scores, noting, "So, we are now down to the decision between gold and silver in the men's long jump." The scene shifts to Jarrion Lawson, in his blue Nike singlet with 'USA' and the American flag, standing on the track with a focused expression. The commentator identifies him as "Jarrion Lawson of the United States," confirming his 8.43m score from "the second round" and noting he's "5 centimeters adrift of Manyonga." The brightly lit stadium, adorned with 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017', 'TOYOTA', 'TDK', and 'Newham London' banners, buzzes with crowd noise, as spectators like the Man in Blue Hoodie with 'AMERICAN FREEDOM' and the Man in Teal 'AUSTRALIA' Jacket react to the tense, competitive atmosphere.

The segment opens with a close-up of Jarrion Lawson, the American long jumper in his blue Nike singlet with 'USA' and the American flag, intensely focused as he bends forward, with a commentator speculating he "could produce an even bigger jump if he has to" and has "got it all before him here for the gold." The scoreboard displays his previous 8.43m (SB) mark, placing him second. As Lawson performs his characteristic 'shaking legs' and head-back routine, the commentator notes he "gets the crowd going," and the stadium crowd's roar is clearly audible. The commentator praises the "wonderful competition" and "great performances," even mentioning "Manyonga helping him out well," highlighting "sportsmanship." Lawson rises, waves to the roaring crowd, and begins his powerful, fluid run-up along the red track for his "last attempt." The commentator builds anticipation, stating he's "in silver medal position" and then exclaiming, "Fast down the runway!" As Lawson approaches the white and blue takeoff board, he leaps into the sandpit with a strong, controlled motion, landing with a splash of sand. The commentator immediately reacts, "It's the board, and it's a big jump!" and then, "Whoa, this is going to be a white flag goes up," as a female official in a white shirt raises the white flag, signaling a valid jump. Lawson walks away, smiling and raising his arms in celebration, acknowledging the massive, clapping, and phone-recording Stadium Crowd, while the commentator declares, "What a great last jump!" The camera pans to a wide shot of the vibrant stadium under bright lights, with 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'TDK' banners visible along the trackside. The commentator then builds suspense, stating Lawson "has got to get 8.48," and after a moment of "looking," excitedly announces, "8.44! Wow! A big, big jump!" The segment concludes with a brief shot of Luvo Manyonga, in his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, kneeling on the track in a moment of focus or recovery.

The segment opens with Ruswahl Samaai, the South African long jumper in a green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, celebrating his bronze medal with a raised right arm and a dynamic split on the track, having secured third place with 8.32m, as commentators confirm, "Samaai has got the bronze." The focus then shifts to Jarrion Lawson, the American long jumper in a blue Nike singlet, walking confidently with the U.S. flag draped over his shoulder, his bib reading 'LAWSON LONDON 2017'. The scoreboard confirms his second-place finish with an 8.44m Season Best, and commentators praise his "fine effort" and "silver medal" performance, while "pyrotechnics are going off in the stadium" adding to the celebratory atmosphere. The scene transitions to Luvo Manyonga, the South African long jumper in a green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet, preparing for his jump. Commentary highlights his journey, noting he "has turned his life around with athletics," winning the World Junior title in 2010 and an Olympic silver last year, while also revealing his intense anxiety, stating he "couldn't bear to look" and "he's crying." The tension builds as commentators ask, "can he finish with a bang?" Manyonga executes a powerful run-up, leaping into the sandpit with a splash, his red and blue shoes prominent. Officials in white shirts measure the jump and raise a white flag, confirming its validity amidst a "massive" jump and a "crowd roar." The stadium is packed with a vibrant, cheering crowd under bright lights, with banners like 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and 'TOYOTA' visible. The scoreboard ultimately displays the final standings: Manyonga 8.48m (1st), Lawson 8.44m (2nd), and Samaai 8.32m (3rd), as commentators exclaim, "Oh, it is! It's massive!" and "He really did, he did finish with a bang!" Manyonga walks away from the sandpit, his emotional journey culminating in securing the gold medal.

The video captures the triumphant aftermath of Luvo Manyonga’s gold medal-winning long jump at the IAAF World Championships London 2017. After landing in the sandpit with a powerful splash, Manyonga rises and begins celebrating his victory, his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet with 'MANYONGA' and 'LONDON 2017' on the bib clearly visible. An unseen commentator exclaims, "Oh, wow, wonderful, wonderful competition and a great gold medal for South Africa here," noting Manyonga "came in as the favorite and he really did produce the goods." The on-screen graphic confirms Manyonga’s win with a jump of 8.48m, labeled 'WINNER LONG JUMP MEN' and 'GOLD'. He is joined by his fellow South African teammate Ruswahl Samaai, also in a matching green and yellow singlet with 'SAMAAL' on his bib. The two athletes embrace warmly, sharing a moment of national pride as the commentator highlights "gold and bronze for South Africa" and "their first two medals," describing them as "two teammates and the two good friends." They are surrounded by photographers and media personnel, including a man in a black vest and another in a purple shirt, actively capturing the historic moment. The athletes then walk along the track, waving to the massive, cheering crowd in the brightly lit stadium, with Manyonga draped in the South African flag. They high-five each other and gesture to the audience, their expressions filled with joy and relief. Trackside banners for 'TOYOTA' and 'TDK' are visible, along with the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' signage. The commentator then reflects on Manyonga's inspiring journey, stating, "Manyonga's story shows you are never too late to turn your life around," detailing how he "made some bad decisions," "spent some time in jail," but "went back to the drawing board" and "back to athletics, what he loved," after previously finishing fifth in Daegu.

The segment opens with an elated Luvo Manyonga, the South African long jumper, celebrating his gold medal victory at the IAAF World Championships London 2017. Draped in the South African flag, he waves it triumphantly on the red track, his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet visible, as a commentator describes how he "drove him" and is now "raising the flag for the Rainbow Nation." The commentator hails him as the "Olympic silver medalist" who is now the "World Champion," emphasizing "what a story of redemption." Manyonga, with a broad smile, high-fives teammate Ruswahl Samaai, who is also in a matching singlet. The on-screen graphic confirms his win with a jump of 8.48m, marked as 'GOLD'. The scene transitions to a wide-angle view of the packed stadium, where the full scoreboard for the Long Jump Men's final is displayed. The commentator provides "confirmation of that wonderful long jump," detailing "Luvo Manyonga with the gold medal, 8.48." He then notes "Jarrion Lawson tried his best as he could with a season's best 8.44 and silver medal," and "Ruswahl Samaai getting that all-important bronze medal for South Africa." The scoreboard lists Manyonga in first place with 8.48m, followed by Jarrion Lawson (USA) with 8.44m (SB), and Ruswahl Samaai (RSA) with 8.32m. The stadium crowd, under bright lights, erupts in loud cheers and applause, with many spectators recording the moment on their phones, creating a vibrant and celebratory atmosphere. Banners for IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017, TOYOTA, and SEIKO are visible, underscoring the professional event.
